perturbation.original_text: 14. This Agreement shall come into force on the Effective Date and shall continue in full force and effect, notwithstanding the completion of the Purpose, for a period of seven years from the Effective Date unless extended, superseded or otherwise varied by a subsequent written agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. This Agreement shall come into force on the Effective Date and shall continue in full force and effect, notwithstanding the completion of the Purpose, for a period of twenty years from the Effective Date unless extended, superseded or otherwise varied by a subsequent written agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality period to 20 years may be seen as an unreasonable length of time. This can cause enforceability issues, particularly in jurisdictions which consider limitations on trade and unreasonable restraints. The standard is normally 5-10 years depending on the industry and the details.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' A twenty-year non-disclosure agreement may be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it covers a broad range of information, making it potentially unenforceable in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality period from seven years to twenty years. The Law Explanation states this may violate California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 quote BPC 16600, stating that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' A twenty-year non-disclosure agreement could be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade, creating a legal contradiction with BPC 16600 because such a lengthy restriction could impede someone's ability to work in their field.

perturbation.original_text: d. at the request and direction of the Disclosing Party, and without delay, return or destroy any Information provided to it pursuant to this Agreement and any copies of such Information, except that one copy may be kept by the Receiving Party for arc hival purposes and for the purpose of defending itself against any claims arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: d. at the request and direction of the Disclosing Party, and within 90 days, return or destroy any Information provided to it pursuant to this Agreement and any copies of such Information, except that one copy may be kept by the Receiving Party for archival purposes and for the purpose of defending itself against any claims arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'without delay' to 'within 90 days' contradicts common law expectations for the return of confidential information upon request. Immediate return is usually implied.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Agency § 396 states that an agent (here, the Receiving Party) has a duty to surrender to the principal (Disclosing Party) items entrusted to them, which includes confidential information. Delaying the return of this information for 90 days could be a breach of this duty, especially if the Disclosing Party needs the information immediately.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to return or destroy information 'without delay,' implying immediate action upon request. The changed text introduces a 90-day window, which directly contradicts the expectation of immediate return. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this contradicts common law expectations, and the Law Explanation highlights the duty to surrender items promptly, suggesting that a 90-day delay could be a breach. While the scraped snippets provide limited direct support, the analysis provided indicates that a reasonable legal expectation for the return of confidential information is immediate, making the 90-day delay a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. The Parties agree that the ETI may disclose that one or more of the Respondents are involved in discussions with the ETI and the subject matter of the discussions provided that the ETI will provide a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents and seek the approva l of the Respondents prior to its publication or release. Other than as set out in this clause, none of the Parties will make any public announcements, statements or otherwise publicise the subject matter of this Agreement (or its existence) without the pr ior written consent of the other Parties and no Party will use the business names or trade marks of any other Party in any way without that Party’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. The Parties agree that the ETI may disclose that one or more of the Respondents are involved in discussions with the ETI and the subject matter of the discussions. Other than as set out in this clause, none of the Parties will make any public announcements, statements or otherwise publicise the subject matter of this Agreement (or its existence) without the prior written consent of the other Parties and no Party will use the business names or trade marks of any other Party in any way without that Party’s prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause about the ETI providing a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents and seeking their approval prior to its publication violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that 'Every contract or duty within [the Uniform Commercial Code] imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By removing the requirement for ETI to seek approval before public disclosure, there's a potential breach of this implied duty of good faith, as it could unfairly harm the Respondents' interests or reputation. While this contract may not be fully governed by the UCC, the principle of good faith is often applied in contract interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text requires ETI to provide a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents and seek their approval prior to its publication or release. The changed text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation states that this removal could breach the implied duty of good faith, potentially harming the Respondents' interests or reputation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 1-304, states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' While the contract may not be fully governed by the UCC, the principle of good faith is often applied in contract interpretation, which supports the conclusion that removing the approval requirement creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating this principle.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not disclose or make available the Information by publication o r otherwise to any third party (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement only for carrying out the Purpose;
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not disclose or make available the Information by publication o r otherwise to any third party (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and may use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement for carrying out the Purpose, or any other purpose it deems reasonable;
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the purpose for which the receiving party can use the information to "any other purpose it deems reasonable" contradicts the core principle of confidentiality agreements, undermining the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) defines misappropriation as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. Allowing the Receiving Party to use confidential information for 'any other purpose it deems reasonable' effectively eliminates the protection of trade secrets, as it permits uses beyond the scope of the agreed-upon purpose and thus constitutes a misappropriation.
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Receiving Party to use confidential information for 'any other purpose it deems reasonable,' which directly contradicts the principle of protecting trade secrets as explained in the Law Explanation. While no scraped snippets are available, the Law Explanation states that this modification effectively eliminates the protection of trade secrets, as it permits uses beyond the scope of the agreed-upon purpose, which constitutes a misappropriation. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the changed text introduces a purpose clause that weakens legal protection.

perturbation.original_text: 4. If a Receiving Party believes it is required by law to disclose any Information under clause 3(d) above, the Receiving Party shall (in each case and to the extent not prohibited in law): a. provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of such requirement or obligation (together with a copy of any relevant access request, court order or other evidence giving rise Non-Disclosure Agreement Energy Technologies Institute © 2016 Energy Technologies Institute LLP. The information in this document is the property of Energy Technologies Institute LLP. www.eti.co.uk Delivering the UK’s Future Energy Technologies 4 to such belief) in advance of the required disclosure, to enable the Disclosing Party to seek appropriate protective relief and/or to take other steps to resist or narrow the scope of any required disclosure; b. where it is not permitted in law to notify the requirement for disclosure in advance of the required disclosure, notify the Disclosing Party as soon as reasonably practicable after the disclosure confirming the nature of and extent of the disclosure; and c. co-operate with the Disclosing Party with respect to such matters, and in any event disclose only such Information as it has ascertained, after taking legal advice, it is legally co mpelled to disclose.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. If a Receiving Party believes it is required by law to disclose any Information under clause 3(d) above, the Receiving Party shall (in each case and to the extent not prohibited in law): a. provide the Disclosing Party with notice of such requirement or obligation (together with a copy of any relevant access request, court order or other evidence giving rise to such belief) at the same time as the information is disclosed; b. where it is not permitted in law to notify the requirement for disclosure in advance of the required disclosure, notify the Disclosing Party as soon as reasonably practicable after the disclosure confirming the nature of and extent of the disclosure; and c. co-operate with the Disclosing Party with respect to such matters, and in any event disclose only such Information as it has ascertained, after taking legal advice, it is legally compelled to disclose.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word "prompt" and replacing "in advance of the required disclosure" with "at the same time as the information is disclosed" violates the principle that the disclosing party is given enough time to seek legal relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) implies the need for timely notice and opportunity to object to a subpoena for information. By providing notice only "at the same time as the information is disclosed," the disclosing party is not afforded the opportunity to challenge the subpoena or seek a protective order prior to the disclosure, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modification, by changing the notice requirement to 'at the same time as the information is disclosed' from 'prompt written notice...in advance of the required disclosure,' directly contradicts the principle of timely notice as it relates to legal proceedings. The Law Explanation points to FRCP 45(d)(2)(B), which implies an opportunity to object to a subpoena. While 'Scraped Law Snippet 1' (Rule 45. Subpoena) doesn't explicitly state 'timely,' the rule inherently suggests a period before disclosure to allow for compliance and potential legal challenges. Giving notice simultaneously negates the possibility of seeking protective relief before disclosure, thus creating a legal contradiction by undermining the process for challenging subpoenas as described by the LLM's Law Explanation.
